criteria.criterion: The paper presents a benchmark study.
criteria.fact: This paper offers a comprehensive benchmark study

criteria.criterion: The benchmark study includes centralized machine learning algorithms.
criteria.fact: centralized ML algorithms

criteria.fact: federated ML algorithms

criteria.criterion: The evaluated algorithms are intended for heart disease classification.
criteria.fact: for heart disease classification

criteria.criterion: The study uses the UCI Heart Disease dataset (reference [1]).
criteria.fact: using the UCI heart disease dataset [1]

criteria.criterion: Data in the UCI heart disease dataset were collected from multiple geographically distant hospitals.
criteria.fact: data across geographically distant hospitals

criteria.criterion: The dataset contains naturally heterogeneous splits (differences in data distribution) across those hospitals.
criteria.fact: naturally heterogeneous splits of the data across geographically distant hospitals

criteria.fact: provides a great example of naturally heterogeneous splits

criteria.criterion: The contribution is a centralized benchmark.
criteria.fact: Centralized benchmark

criteria.criterion: The benchmark trains binary classification algorithms.
criteria.fact: Train various binary classification algorithms

criteria.criterion: The benchmark trains logistic regression (LR) for binary classification.
criteria.fact: Logistic regression (LR) is trained

criteria.criterion: The benchmark trains neural networks (NN) for binary classification.
criteria.fact: Neural networks (NN) are trained

criteria.criterion: The benchmark trains support vector machines (SVM) for binary classification.
criteria.fact: Support vector machines (SVM) are trained

criteria.criterion: The benchmark trains k-nearest neighbor (KNN) for binary classification.
criteria.fact: K-nearest neighbor (KNN) is trained

criteria.criterion: The benchmark trains Naïve Bayes (NB) for binary classification.
criteria.fact: Naïve Bayes (NB) is trained

criteria.criterion: The benchmark trains random forests for binary classification.
criteria.fact: Random forests are trained

criteria.criterion: There is a centralized setup in which model performance is evaluated and models can be ranked by performance.
criteria.fact: models are evaluated and ranked by performance in the centralized setup

criteria.criterion: Select the top three performing models from the centralized setup.
criteria.fact: the top three performing models are identified in the centralized setup

criteria.criterion: Use the selected top three models as local trainers in the federated setting.
criteria.fact: the top three performing models from the centralized setup are used as local trainers

criteria.fact: To meet our specific needs, we develop our own version of federated SVM from scratch.

criteria.criterion: Ensure the federated SVM implementation is tailored to and satisfies our specified requirements and constraints.
criteria.fact: To meet our specific needs, we develop our own version of federated SVM from scratch.

criteria.criterion: Retain ownership and maintenance responsibility for the implemented federated SVM (it is 'our own version').
criteria.fact: To meet our specific needs, we develop our own version of federated SVM from scratch.

criteria.fact: Computing SHAP values for the centralized models highlights four features that are most important factors for predictions.

criteria.criterion: <fact>In this work, all of the four clients are selected in each federation round, i.e., no client sampling.</fact> <scope>from paper context</scope>
criteria.fact: In this work, all of the four clients are selected in each federation round, i.e., no client sampling.

criteria.criterion: <fact>Suppose we have $K$ clients, where client $k$ has $n _ { k }$ data points, and the total number of data points is $n$ , then the aggregated model at any federation round $t$ is given by  $$ \theta^ {(t)} = \sum_ {k = 1} ^ {K} \frac {n _ {k}}{n} \theta_ {k} ^ {(t)} $$  where $\theta _ { k } ^ { ( t ) }$ (k are the trained model parameters at client $k$ and federation round $t$ .</fact> <scope>from paper context</scope>
criteria.fact: Suppose we have $K$ clients, where client $k$ has $n _ { k }$ data points, and the total number of data points is $n$ , then the aggregated model at any federation round $t$ is given by  $$ \theta^ {(t)} = \sum_ {k = 1} ^ {K} \frac {n _ {k}}{n} \theta_ {k} ^ {(t)} $$  where $\theta _ { k } ^ { ( t ) }$ (k are the trained model parameters at client $k$ and federation round $t$ .

criteria.criterion: <fact>In this work, as in [7], local updates in batches are counted rather than local epochs, aligning with theoretical formulations and extending to related strategies based on FedAvg.</fact> <scope>from paper context</scope>
criteria.fact: In this work, as in [7], local updates in batches are counted rather than local epochs, aligning with theoretical formulations and extending to related strategies based on FedAvg.

criteria.criterion: <fact>FedAdam [16] is an extension of the FedAvg algorithm that incorporates the ADAM [47] optimizer for improved performance.</fact> <scope>from paper context</scope>
criteria.fact: FedAdam [16] is an extension of the FedAvg algorithm that incorporates the ADAM [47] optimizer for improved performance.

criteria.criterion: <fact>By dynamically adjusting  # Algorithm 1 Local classifier  Require: Dataset $D$ (920 points)  1: Set hyperparameter values for chosen model    2: Remove points with missing values from $D$    3: Split $D$ into $66 \%$ training set $T$ and $34 \%$ test set $E$    4: Normalize $T$ (e.g., scale features to have zero mean and unit variance)    5: Initialize model parameters $\theta$    6: for $t = 1$ to 50 epochs do    7: Update model parameters $\theta$ by training on $T$    8: end for    9: Evaluate final model on test set $E$    10: Compute testing accuracy Acc    11: Output Acc as the final testing accuracy  # Algorithm 2 Federated aggregation  Require: Dataset D from 4 clients $( D _ { 1 } , D _ { 2 } , D _ { 3 } , D _ { 4 } )$  1: Set hyperparameter values for chosen model    2: Initialize global model parameters $\theta$    3: for each client $k \in \{ 1 , 2 , 3 , 4 \}$ do    4: Preprocess data similar to Local Classifier    5: for $t = 1$ to 50 epochs do    6: Local Classifier    7: Send $\theta _ { k }$ to the server    8: Receive updated global model parameters $\theta$ from the server    9: end for    10: Evaluate local model on test set $E _ { k }$    11: Compute local testing accuracy $A c c _ { k }$    12: end for    13: Aggregate local testing accuracies $A c c _ { 1 } , A c c _ { 2 } , A c c _ { 3 } , A c c _ { 4 }$    14: PCompute average testing accuracy4k=1 nkn Acck $\begin{array} { r l } { \operatorname { A c c } _ { \mathrm { a v g } } \quad } & { { } = } \end{array}$ -nAcck    15: Output $\mathbf { A c c _ { a v g } }$ as the final testing accuracy  learning rates based on the magnitude and direction of gradients, ADAM optimizes the training process for each client individually, contributing to more accurate model updates.</fact> <scope>from paper context</scope>
criteria.fact: By dynamically adjusting  # Algorithm 1 Local classifier  Require: Dataset $D$ (920 points)  1: Set hyperparameter values for chosen model    2: Remove points with missing values from $D$    3: Split $D$ into $66 \%$ training set $T$ and $34 \%$ test set $E$    4: Normalize $T$ (e.g., scale features to have zero mean and unit variance)    5: Initialize model parameters $\theta$    6: for $t = 1$ to 50 epochs do    7: Update model parameters $\theta$ by training on $T$    8: end for    9: Evaluate final model on test set $E$    10: Compute testing accuracy Acc    11: Output Acc as the final testing accuracy  # Algorithm 2 Federated aggregation  Require: Dataset D from 4 clients $( D _ { 1 } , D _ { 2 } , D _ { 3 } , D _ { 4 } )$  1: Set hyperparameter values for chosen model    2: Initialize global model parameters $\theta$    3: for each client $k \in \{ 1 , 2 , 3 , 4 \}$ do    4: Preprocess data similar to Local Classifier    5: for $t = 1$ to 50 epochs do    6: Local Classifier    7: Send $\theta _ { k }$ to the server    8: Receive updated global model parameters $\theta$ from the server    9: end for    10: Evaluate local model on test set $E _ { k }$    11: Compute local testing accuracy $A c c _ { k }$    12: end for    13: Aggregate local testing accuracies $A c c _ { 1 } , A c c _ { 2 } , A c c _ { 3 } , A c c _ { 4 }$    14: PCompute average testing accuracy4k=1 nkn Acck $\begin{array} { r l } { \operatorname { A c c } _ { \mathrm { a v g } } \quad } & { { } = } \end{array}$ -nAcck    15: Output $\mathbf { A c c _ { a v g } }$ as the final testing accuracy  learning rates based on the magnitude and direction of gradients, ADAM optimizes the training process for each client individually, contributing to more accurate model updates.

criteria.criterion: <fact>Client updates are computed as:  $$ \begin{array}{l} \Delta_ {t} = \frac {1}{| S |} \sum_ {i \in S} \Delta_ {i} ^ {t} \\ m _ {t} = \beta_ {1} m _ {t - 1} + (1 - \beta_ {1}) \Delta_ {t} \tag {1} \\ v _ {t} = \beta_ {2} v _ {t - 1} + (1 - \beta_ {2}) (\Delta_ {t}) ^ {2} \\ x _ {t + 1} = x _ {t} - \eta \frac {m _ {t}}{\sqrt {v _ {t}} + \tau} \\ \end{array} $$  where $\Delta _ { t }$ is the aggregated gradient update at time t, $S$ is the set of clients participating, $| S |$ is the number of clients in $S$ , $m _ { t }$ and $v _ { t }$ are the first and second moment estimates, $\beta _ { 1 }$ and $\beta _ { 2 }$ are the exponential decay rates for these moment estimates, $\eta$ is the learning rate, and $\tau$ is a small constant for numerical stability.</fact> <scope>from paper context</scope>
criteria.fact: Client updates are computed as:  $$ \begin{array}{l} \Delta_ {t} = \frac {1}{| S |} \sum_ {i \in S} \Delta_ {i} ^ {t} \\ m _ {t} = \beta_ {1} m _ {t - 1} + (1 - \beta_ {1}) \Delta_ {t} \tag {1} \\ v _ {t} = \beta_ {2} v _ {t - 1} + (1 - \beta_ {2}) (\Delta_ {t}) ^ {2} \\ x _ {t + 1} = x _ {t} - \eta \frac {m _ {t}}{\sqrt {v _ {t}} + \tau} \\ \end{array} $$  where $\Delta _ { t }$ is the aggregated gradient update at time t, $S$ is the set of clients participating, $| S |$ is the number of clients in $S$ , $m _ { t }$ and $v _ { t }$ are the first and second moment estimates, $\beta _ { 1 }$ and $\beta _ { 2 }$ are the exponential decay rates for these moment estimates, $\eta$ is the learning rate, and $\tau$ is a small constant for numerical stability.

criteria.criterion: <fact>FedYogi [16] is designed to be a more adaptive and efficient variant of FedAvg, leveraging techniques to handle non-IID data distributions and optimize communication for improved federated aggregation.</fact> <scope>from paper context</scope>
criteria.fact: FedYogi [16] is designed to be a more adaptive and efficient variant of FedAvg, leveraging techniques to handle non-IID data distributions and optimize communication for improved federated aggregation.

criteria.criterion: <fact>For each client, the updates are computed similarly to FedAdam, except for the second moment estimate, $v _ { t }$ , which is computed as  $$ v _ {t} = v _ {t - 1} - (1 - \beta_ {2}) \Delta_ {t} ^ {2} \mathrm {s i g n} (v _ {t - 1} - \Delta_ {t} ^ {2}) $$  where $v _ { t } , \beta _ { 2 } , \Delta _ { t }$ are the same as in (1).</fact> <scope>from paper context</scope>
criteria.fact: For each client, the updates are computed similarly to FedAdam, except for the second moment estimate, $v _ { t }$ , which is computed as  $$ v _ {t} = v _ {t - 1} - (1 - \beta_ {2}) \Delta_ {t} ^ {2} \mathrm {s i g n} (v _ {t - 1} - \Delta_ {t} ^ {2}) $$  where $v _ { t } , \beta _ { 2 } , \Delta _ { t }$ are the same as in (1).

criteria.criterion: <fact>Option (ii) reuses the previously computed gradients, making it the cheaper but less stable option.</fact> <scope>from paper context</scope>
criteria.fact: Option (ii) reuses the previously computed gradients, making it the cheaper but less stable option.

criteria.fact: It measures the deviation of the ST segment on an electrocardiogram (ECG), which can indicate ischemia, which is reduced blood flow to the heart.

criteria.criterion: <fact>CONCLUSION  In this work, we established a benchmark for binary classification models for heart disease using the UCI heart disease dataset, evaluated in both centralized (pooled) and federated settings, and conducted interpretability analysis using Shapley values to understand the features influencing model predictions.</fact> <scope>from paper context</scope>
criteria.fact: CONCLUSION  In this work, we established a benchmark for binary classification models for heart disease using the UCI heart disease dataset, evaluated in both centralized (pooled) and federated settings, and conducted interpretability analysis using Shapley values to understand the features influencing model predictions.

criteria.criterion: <fact>In the centralized setting, a linear kernel SVM achieves the highest accuracy of $8 3 .</fact> <scope>from paper context</scope>
criteria.fact: In the centralized setting, a linear kernel SVM achieves the highest accuracy of $8 3 .

criteria.criterion: <fact>In the federated setting, we trained various local training algorithms and model aggregation strategies.</fact> <scope>from paper context</scope>
criteria.fact: In the federated setting, we trained various local training algorithms and model aggregation strategies.

criteria.criterion: <fact>The best performing model was an SVM local trainer with testing accuracy $7 3 .</fact> <scope>from paper context</scope>
criteria.fact: The best performing model was an SVM local trainer with testing accuracy $7 3 .

criteria.criterion: <fact>8 \%$ for FedAdam and FedYogi aggregation strategies.</fact> <scope>from paper context</scope>
criteria.fact: 8 \%$ for FedAdam and FedYogi aggregation strategies.
